pages.title: EXPOSÉ II TANGENT BUNDLES – LIE ALGEBRAS

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ II
TANGENT BUNDLES – LIE ALGEBRASby M. Demazure

We propose in this exposé to construct the analogue in the theory of schemes of the tangent bundles and Lie algebras of the classical theory. It will however be useful not to restrict ourselves to schemes proper, but to consider functors on the category of schemes which are not necessarily representable (for example the functors \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}\), etc…). As was announced in the preceding exposé I, 1.1, we shall identify a scheme with the functor associated to it.

On the other hand, the constructions presented below go beyond the framework of the theory of schemes. They are equally valid, for example, in the theory of analytic spaces with nilpotent elements, up to a few modifications of detail.
Before beginning this construction, we must set down some general definitions which complete those of I, 1.7.
1. The functors \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)\)

Let us take up again the notation of I, 1.1. We identify the category \(\mathscr{C}\) with a full subcategory of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}} = \mathrm{Hom}(\mathscr{C}^{\circ}, (\mathrm{Ens}))\) (in particular we drop the underlines which allowed us to distinguish graphically an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) from an object of \(\mathscr{C}\)).

Consider the following situation: four objects of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), denoted S, X, Y, Z, the first being in fact an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), X and Y over Z, Z over S: \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[dr]_-{p_X} & & Y \ar[dl]^-{p_Y} \\ & Z \ar[d] & \\ & S & } \]
Let us then define an object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)\), by \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)(S') = \mathrm{Hom}_{Z_{S'}}(X_{S'}, Y_{S'}). \]

One sees at once that \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)\) is none other than the subobject of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)\) formed by the morphisms compatible with \(p_X\) and \(p_Y\), that is, the kernel of the pair of morphisms \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y) \rightrightarrows \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Z) \] defined, the first by composition with \(p_Y\), the second as being the constant morphism whose "image" is \(p_X\). It follows from the definition that one has isomorphisms \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(S', \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_Z(X\times_S S', Y) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_Z(Z\times_S S', \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_Z(X,Y)) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_Z(X, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_Z(Z\times_S S', Y)). \] Let us note in passing that the last two isomorphisms extend to the case where \(S'\) is no longer in \(\mathscr{C}\) by the procedure indicated in I, 1.7.1.

Let us point out two special cases of the definition. If \(Z=S\), we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S/S}(X,Y) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y). \] On the other hand, we set \[ \prod_{Z/S} Y = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(Z,Y), \] so that by definition \[ \Bigl(\prod_{Z/S} Y\Bigr)(S') \simeq \Gamma(Y_{S'}/Z_{S'}). \]

Let us also note that one has an isomorphism \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{X/S}(X, Y\underset{Z}{\times} X) = \prod_{X/S} Y\underset{Z}{\times} X, \] which gives in particular for \(Z=S\) an isomorphism \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y) \simeq \prod_{X/S} Y_X. \]

One last remark: the functor \(Y \rightsquigarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)\) commutes with products in the following sense: one has a functorial isomorphism \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X, Y\underset{Z}{\times} Y') \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y) \underset{S}{\times} \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y'). \] It follows that if Y is a Z-group, resp. a Z-ring, …, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)\) is an S-group, resp. an S-ring, ….
2. The schemes \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\)

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1

Let S be a scheme and M a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module. We denote by \(\mathrm{D}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(M)\) the quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebra \(\mathscr{O}_S \oplus M\) (where M is considered as an ideal of square zero). We denote by \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\) the S-scheme \(\mathrm{Spec}\, \mathrm{D}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(M)\). In particular we write \(\mathrm{D}_{\mathscr{O}_S} = \mathrm{D}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{O}_S)\), \(\mathrm{I}_S = \mathrm{I}_S(\mathscr{O}_S)\) and call them respectively the algebra of dual numbers over S and the scheme of dual numbers over S.

Then \(M \mapsto \mathrm{I}_S(M)\) is a contravariant functor from the category of quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules to that of S-schemes. In particular the morphisms \(0 \to M\) and \(M \to 0\) define respectively the structural morphism \(\rho : \mathrm{I}_S(M) \to \mathrm{I}_S(0) = S\) and a section \(\varepsilon_M\) of the latter, which is called the zero section.

On the other hand \(\mathbf{O}(S) = \Gamma(S, \mathscr{O}_S)\) is a set of operators of the module M, hence of the S-scheme \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\), and the operations of \(\mathbf{O}(S)\) commute with the S-morphisms \(\mathrm{I}_S(M) \to \mathrm{I}_S(M')\) coming from morphisms \(M' \to M\) by the preceding functoriality; in particular these operations preserve the zero section of \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\).

The formation of the \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\) commutes with base extension: one has canonical isomorphisms \[ (\mathrm{I}_S(M))_{S'} \simeq \mathrm{I}_{S'}(M \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{O}_{S'}). \] Now let M and N be two quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules. The commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & M\oplus N \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ M \ar[dr] & & N \ar[dl] \\ & 0 & } \] defines a commutative diagram of S-schemes \[ (*) \qquad \xymatrix{ & \mathrm{I}_S(M\oplus N) \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ \mathrm{I}_S(M) \ar[dr]_-{\varepsilon_M} & & \mathrm{I}_S(N) \ar[dl]^-{\varepsilon_N} \\ & S & } \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2

For every S-scheme X, the diagram of functors over S obtained by applying the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(-,X)\) to the diagram (\(*\)) is cartesian: \[ \xymatrix{ & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{I}_S(M\oplus N), X) \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{I}_S(M), X) \ar[dr] & & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{I}_S(N), X) \ar[dl] \\ & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(S,X) = X & } \]
We must check that for every \(S' \to S\), the diagram of sets obtained by taking the values of the functors on \(S'\) is cartesian. Since the formation of \(\mathrm{I}_S(P)\) commutes with

base extension in the sense made explicit above, it suffices to do it for \(S'=S\), hence to check that the following diagram of sets is cartesian: \[ \xymatrix{ & X(\mathrm{I}_S(M\oplus N)) \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ X(\mathrm{I}_S(M)) \ar[dr]_-{X(\varepsilon_M)} & & X(\mathrm{I}_S(N)) \ar[dl]^-{X(\varepsilon_N)} \\ & X(S) & } \] Now, if \(x \in X(S)\), it follows from (SGA 1, III 5.1) that \(X(\varepsilon_M)^{-1}(x)\) is isomorphic, functorially in M, to \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(x^*(\Omega^1_{X/S}), M)\), where \(\Omega^1_{X/S}\) denotes the sheaf of relative differentials of X with respect to S. Now this last functor (in M) evidently transforms a direct sum of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules into the product of the corresponding sets, whence the result.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

If M is free of finite type, the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{I}_S(M), X)\) is isomorphic (as a functor over X) to a finite product (over X) of copies of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{I}_S, X)\).

blocks.title: Nota
blocks.html:
Nota

It follows from the proof of the proposition that \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{I}_S, X)\) is isomorphic as an X-functor to \(\mathbf{V}(\Omega^1_{X/S})\) (I, 4.6.1), hence representable by the vector bundle \(\mathbf{V}(\Omega^1_{X/S})\).
3. The tangent bundle, the condition (E)

In this section, unless otherwise stated, the letters M, \(M'\), N … will always denote \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules free of finite type (that is, isomorphic to a finite direct sum of copies of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)). We shall systematically use the identifications justified in Exposé I; thus we shall say "functor over S" to denote indifferently a functor equipped with a morphism to S (\(= h_S\)) or a functor on the category of objects over S. We shall likewise say "group functor over S" …

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

Let S be a scheme and M an \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module free of finite type. Let X be a functor over S. We call the tangent bundle of X over S relative to the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module M, and denote by \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\), the S-functor \[ \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{I}_S(M), X). \] In particular, we call the tangent bundle of X over S, and denote by \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}\), the functor \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S} = \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(\mathscr{O}_S) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{I}_S, X)\).

Then \(M \mapsto \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) is a covariant functor from the category of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules free of finite type to the category of S-functors. In particular the morphisms \(0 \to M\) and \(M \to 0\) define respectively an S-morphism \[ \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) \longrightarrow \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(0) \simeq X \] and a section of the latter, called the zero section.

It follows from this that \(M \mapsto \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) is a covariant functor from the category of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules free of finite type to that of functors over X. In particular \(\mathbf{O}(S)\) is a set of operators of the X-functor \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) which respects "the functoriality in M".

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.2

Let \(u \in X(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_S(S,X) = \Gamma(X/S)\). We call the tangent space of X over S at the point u relative to M, and denote by \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\), the S-functor obtained from the X-functor \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) by pullback along the morphism \(u : S \to X\): \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) \ar[d] \\ S \ar[r]_-{u} & X } \] In particular \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(\mathscr{O}_S)\) is denoted \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}\). It is the tangent space of X over S at the point u.
Let us note at once the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3

If X is representable by an S-scheme denoted \(\widetilde{X}\), then \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) and \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\) are representable. In particular \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}\) and \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}\) are representable by vector bundles over \(\widetilde{X}\) and over S, which are respectively \(\mathbf{V}(\Omega^1_{\widetilde{X}/S})\) and \(\mathbf{V}(u^*(\Omega^1_{\widetilde{X}/S}))\).

It obviously suffices to prove the proposition for \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\), the analogous results for \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\) being deduced from it by pullback. By 2.2, corollary, it even suffices to do it for \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}\), and in that case it is none other than the remark pointed out in the note after 2.2. From this proposition results a particularly simple description of the vector bundle representing \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}\): the image of the section u of X over S is locally closed, hence defined by a quasi-coherent ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of a scheme induced on an open subset of X. The quotient \(\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2\) may be considered as a quasi-coherent module on S. It is this module which defines the desired vector bundle.

Let for example X be an algebraic scheme over a field k and u a point of X rational over k. Then \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/k} = \mathbf{V}(\mathfrak{t})\) where \(\mathfrak{t}\) is the Zariski tangent k-vector space of the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,u}\).

This parenthesis closed, let us return to the general situation. Let us first remark that \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\) is a covariant functor from the category of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules free of finite
type to that of functors over S. In particular \(\mathbf{O}(S)\) is a set of operators of the S-functor \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\) which respects the functoriality in M.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.4

The formation of \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) and \(\mathrm{L}_{X/S}(M)\) commutes with base extension: let \(S'\) be a scheme over S; one has isomorphisms functorial in M \[ \mathrm{T}_{X_{S'}/S'}(M\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M))_{S'}, \qquad \mathrm{L}^{u'}_{X_{S'}/S'}(M\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M))_{S'}, \qquad u' = (u)_{S'}. \]
This follows immediately from the fact that the \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}\)'s commute with base extension.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

The X-functor \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) (resp. the S-functor \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\)) is naturally equipped with a structure of object with operators \(\mathscr{O}_X\) (resp. \(\mathscr{O}_S\)), this structure being functorial in M.

Let us show it first for \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\). For each \(S'\) over S, \(\mathbf{O}(S')\) operates on \(M\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}\), hence on \(\mathrm{L}^{u'}_{X_{S'}/S'}(M\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}) = \mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)_{S'}\); now one checks that this operation is functorial in \(S'\); it therefore equips \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\), as announced, with a structure of functor with operators \(\mathscr{O}_S\).

For \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) it is a little more complicated. One must equip each \((\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M))_{X'}(X')\) (\(X'\) over X) with a structure of set with set of operators \(\mathbf{O}(X')\) in a manner functorial in \(X'\). For this one constructs the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathrm{T}_{X_{X'}/X'}(M) \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) & & (\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M))_{X'} \ar[ll] \ar[dl] \\ & X_{X'} \ar[dl] & \\ X \ar[dr] & & X' \ar[ul]_-{f'} \ar[ll]^-{f} \ar[dl] \\ & S & } \] where \(X_{X'}\) denotes \(X\times_S X'\) and \(f'\) the section of \(X_{X'}\) over \(X'\) defined by \(f : X' \to X\).

This diagram shows that \((\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M))_{X'}(X')\) is identified with \(\mathrm{L}^{f'}_{X_{X'}/X'}(X')\), on which \(\mathbf{O}(X')\) operates. It only remains to check that this construction is functorial in \(X'\), which presents no difficulty. The isomorphisms of the preceding proposition are then by construction isomorphisms for the structures of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{S'}}\)-objects, resp. \(\mathscr{O}_{S'}\)-objects.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.5

Let S be a scheme and X an S-functor. We say that X satisfies the condition (E) relative to S if, for every \(S'\) over S and all \(\mathscr{O}_{S'}\)-Modules free of finite type M and N, the diagram of sets \[ \xymatrix{ & X(\mathrm{I}_{S'}(M\oplus N)) \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ X(\mathrm{I}_{S'}(M)) & & X(\mathrm{I}_{S'}(N)) \\ & X(S') \ar[ul] \ar[ur] & } \] obtained by applying X to the diagram (\(*\)) defined in 2.1, is cartesian.

It amounts to the same to say that the functor \(M \mapsto \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) transforms direct sums of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules free of finite type into products of X-functors. Proposition 2.2 shows that every representable functor satisfies the condition (E). If X satisfies the condition (E) with respect to S, the functor \(M \mapsto \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\), commuting with products, transforms groups into groups. In particular \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) is a commutative X-group. For the same reason, \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\) is a commutative S-group.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.6

If X/S satisfies (E), the abelian group structure on \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) (resp. \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\)) and the operation of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) (resp. \(\mathscr{O}_S\)) equip \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) (resp. \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\)) with a structure of \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-module (resp. \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module).
The operation of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) (resp. \(\mathscr{O}_S\)) is functorial in M; it therefore respects the abelian group structure, which is deduced by functoriality from that of M.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

If X is representable, in which case, on the one hand it satisfies (E), and on the other hand \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}\) and \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}\) are representable by vector bundles, the preceding laws are the same as those deduced from the vector bundle structures (cf. I, 4.6).

blocks.label: 3.4 bis
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.4 bis

If X satisfies the condition (E) with respect to S, then \(X_{S'}\) satisfies the condition (E) with respect to \(S'\) and the isomorphisms of 3.4 respect the structures of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{S'}}\)-modules, resp. of \(\mathscr{O}_{S'}\)-modules.
No comments needed.
Abbreviation: instead of saying "X satisfies the condition (E) with respect to S", we shall sometimes say "X/S satisfies the condition (E)".

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.7

The functors \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) and \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M)\) are functorial in X: if \(f : X \to X'\) is an S-morphism, one has commutative diagrams: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{T}(f)} \ar[d] & \mathrm{T}_{X'/S}(M) \ar[d] \\ X \ar[r]_-{f} & X' } \qquad \xymatrix{ \mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{L}(f)} & & \mathrm{L}^{f\circ u}_{X'/S}(M) \\ & S \ar[ul] \ar[ur] & } \]

The morphisms \(\mathrm{T}(f)\) and \(\mathrm{L}(f)\) are immediately deduced from the definitions. The commutativity of the diagrams then follows from the functoriality of these morphisms with respect to M and from the fact that \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(0) = X\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

The square above is cartesian when \(f\) is an open immersion, more generally when \(f\) is étale. It defines in general a morphism of X-functors: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) \ar[rr] \ar[dr] & & (\mathrm{T}_{X'/S}(M))_X \ar[dl] \\ & X & } \]

blocks.label: 3.7 bis
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.7 bis

If X and \(X'\) satisfy (E) with respect to S, then \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) \to (\mathrm{T}_{X'/S}(M))_X\) (resp. \(\mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M) \to \mathrm{L}^{f\circ u}_{X'/S}(M)\)) is a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-modules (resp. of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules).
Follows from Proposition 3.7 by functoriality in M.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.8

Let X and Y be two functors over S. One has isomorphisms functorial in M: \[ \mathrm{T}_{X\times_S Y/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) \times_S \mathrm{T}_{Y/S}(M), \] \[ \mathrm{L}^{(u,v)}_{X\times_S Y/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{L}^u_{X/S}(M) \times_S \mathrm{L}^v_{Y/S}(M). \]
Evident from the definitions.

Let us remark that the first isomorphism may also be interpreted as an isomorphism of X × Y-functors \[ \mathrm{T}_{X\times Y/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \bigl(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\underset{X}{\times}(X\times Y)\bigr) \underset{X\times Y}{\times} \bigl(\mathrm{T}_{Y/S}(M)\underset{Y}{\times}(X\times Y)\bigr). \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

If X/S is equipped with an algebraic structure defined by finite cartesian products, then \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) is equipped with a structure of the same species and the projection \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) \to X\) is a morphism of this species of structure.

blocks.label: 3.8 bis
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.8 bis

If X and Y satisfy the condition (E) with respect to S, then \(X\times_S Y\) satisfies it as well and the isomorphisms of 3.8 respect the module structures.

To state the following properties conveniently, let us introduce the following terminology: an H-set is a set equipped with a composition law with two-sided unit; an H-object in a category \(\mathscr{C}\) is defined in the usual way: it is thus an object X of \(\mathscr{C}\), equipped with a morphism \(X\times X \to X\) such that there exists a section of X (over the final object) possessing the properties of a two-sided unit. Every \(\mathscr{C}\)-monoid, in particular every \(\mathscr{C}\)-group, is therefore a \(\mathscr{C}\)-H-object. In particular, an H-object of the category of functors over the scheme S will be called an S-H-functor. If X is an S-H-functor, then \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)\) is an S-H-functor, and if we write \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(X/S,M) = \mathrm{L}^e_{X/S}(M), \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(X/S) = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(X/S,\mathscr{O}_S), \] where e denotes the unit section of X, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(X/S,M)\) is likewise an S-H-functor.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.9

Let X be an S-H-functor satisfying (E) with respect to S. The S-H-functor structure of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(X/S,M)\) coming from that of X coincides with the S-group structure defined in 3.5.1.
It follows from what was said above that \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(X/S,M)\) is an H-object in the category of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules. The proposition will then follow from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.10

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category. Let G be an H-object in the category of \(\mathscr{C}\)-H-objects; G is thus a \(\mathscr{C}\)-H-object (whose composition law we shall denote by \(f : G\times G \to G\)) equipped with a morphism of \(\mathscr{C}\)-H-objects \(h : G\times G \to G\). Then \(f=h\) and f is commutative.

By taking the values of the functors on a variable argument, one reduces in the usual way to checking the lemma when \(\mathscr{C}\) is the category of sets. We thus have a set G and two maps \(f, h : G\times G \to G\) such that \(h(f(x,y),f(z,t)) = f(h(x,z),h(y,t))\). We have on the other hand two elements of G, say e and u, with \(f(e,x) = f(x,e) = x\), \(h(u,x) = h(x,u) = x\). One sees first that \(h(f(u,y),f(x,u)) = h(f(x,u),f(u,y)) = f(x,y)\). In particular, for \(y=e\), we obtain \[ x = f(x,e) = h(f(u,e),f(x,u)) = h(u,f(x,u)) = f(x,u), \] whence, substituting into the original equality, \[ f(x,y) = h(x,y) = h(y,x). \]

blocks.title: Corollary 1
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

If X is an S-H-functor satisfying (E) with respect to S, every element of \(X(\mathrm{I}_S(M))\) which projects onto the unit element of X(S) is invertible.

blocks.title: Corollary 2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

If X is an S-monoid satisfying (E) with respect to S, an element of \(X(\mathrm{I}_S(M))\) is invertible if and only if its image in X(S) is.

blocks.title: Corollary 3
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

If X is an S-group satisfying (E) with respect to S, the two S-group laws on \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(X/S,M)\) coincide.
Before drawing other consequences from the preceding proposition, let us prove another functoriality result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.11

In the situation of Section 1, one has an isomorphism functorial in M \[ \mathrm{T}_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X, \mathrm{T}_{Y/Z}(M)). \]

This follows immediately from the definitions, taking into account the isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(T, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_Z(Z\times_S T, Y)). \]

blocks.title: Corollary 1
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

If Y/Z satisfies (E), then \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Z/S}(X,Y)/S\) satisfies (E) and the isomorphism of 3.11 respects the module structures.

blocks.title: Corollary 2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

One has an isomorphism functorial in M \[ \mathrm{T}_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X, \mathrm{T}_{Y/S}(M))\text{;} \] if Y/S satisfies (E), then \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)/S\) satisfies (E) and the preceding isomorphism respects the structures of \(\mathbf{O}\)-modules over \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)\).

blocks.title: Corollary 3
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

One has an isomorphism functorial in M \[ \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Y/S}(X, \mathrm{T}_{Y/S}(M)), \] where X is considered as a functor over Y by means of \(u : X \to Y\).

blocks.title: Corollary 4
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

One has an isomorphism functorial in M \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_S(X)/S, M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \prod_{X/S} \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M). \]

We shall now interpret geometrically the definition of the tangent bundle. Let T be a scheme over S; one has isomorphisms functorial in M \[ \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)(T) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_S(T, \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)) = \mathrm{Hom}_S(T, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{I}_S(M), X)) \] \[ \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_S(\mathrm{I}_S(M), \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(T,X)) = \mathrm{T}_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(T,X)/S}(M)(S) \] \[ \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}(T_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}, X_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}). \] In particular the morphism \(M \to 0\) gives a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}_S(T, \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)) \ar[d] \ar[r]^-{\sim} & \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}(T_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}, X_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}) \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{Hom}_S(T,X) \ar[r]_-{\text{identity}} & \mathrm{Hom}_S(T,X) } \] where the first vertical arrow is obtained by the projection \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M) \to X\), the second by the base change \(S \to \mathrm{I}_S(M)\). Consequently:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.12

Let T be over X by \(h_0 : T \to X\). The set \(\mathrm{Hom}_X(T, \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M))\) is identified with the set of \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\)-morphisms from \(T_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}\) to \(X_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}\) which restrict to \(h_0\) on \(S \subset \mathrm{I}_S(M)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

The set \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)/X)\) is identified with the set of \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\)-endomorphisms of \(X_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}\) which induce the identity on X.

Let us now remark that this last set is also identified, by the preceding isomorphisms, with \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_S(X)/S, M)(S)\). If X/S satisfies (E), then \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_S(X)/S\) satisfies (E) (prop. 3.11, cor. 2). Applying 3.10, cor. 2 and 3.9, we deduce the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.13

If X/S satisfies (E), every \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\)-endomorphism of \(X_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}\) which induces the identity on X is an automorphism. The group \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M)/X)\) is identified with the group of these automorphisms.

blocks.title: Corollary 1
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

Let \(u : X\to Y\) be an S-isomorphism, Y/S satisfying (E). Every \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\)-morphism from \(X_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}\) to \(Y_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}\) which extends u is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary 2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

If Y/S satisfies (E), the monomorphism \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_S(X,Y) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)\) induces, for every \(u\in \mathrm{Isom}_S(X,Y)\), an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_S(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)/S}(M). \]

blocks.title: Corollary 3
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

If X/S satisfies (E), the monomorphism \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(X) \to \underline{\mathrm{End}}_S(X)\) induces, for every \(u \in \mathrm{Aut}_S(X)\), an isomorphism \(\mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(X)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{End}}_S(X)/S}(M)\). In particular, one has \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(X)/S, M)(S) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_S(X)/S, M)(S) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \prod_{X/S} \mathrm{T}_{X/S}(M). \]

To finish, suppose X representable. We have seen that a section of \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}\) over X is identified with an \(\mathrm{I}_S\)-automorphism of \(X_{\mathrm{I}_S}\) inducing the identity on X. Now \(X_{\mathrm{I}_S}\) and X have the same underlying topological space, the corresponding sheaves of rings being \(\mathrm{D}_{\mathscr{O}_X}\) and \(\mathscr{O}_X\). It follows at once that an infinitesimal automorphism of X is identified with a derivation of the sheaf of rings \(\mathscr{O}_X\) over the sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_S\). We have thus manufactured an isomorphism \[ \Gamma(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}/X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{D\acute{e}r}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{O}_X) \] which, by virtue of 3.13, respects the group structures (and even the \(\mathbf{O}(S)\)-module structures), which recovers the classical interpretation of tangent vector fields in terms of derivations of the structure sheaf. Let us remark moreover that \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}/X)\) is equal to \(\mathrm{H}^0(X, \mathfrak{g}_{X/S})\) where \(\mathfrak{g}_{X/S}\) is the dual of \(\Omega^1_{X/S}\).

pages.title: 4. Tangent space of a group – Lie algebras

blocks.html: 4. Tangent space of a group – Lie algebras

Let G be a group functor over S. We have seen that \(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) are thereby equipped with structures of groups over S. One has group morphisms (3.1) \[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M) \ar[r]^-{i} & \mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M) \ar@<.6ex>[r]^-{p} & G \ar@<.6ex>[l]^-{s} } \] by definition i is an isomorphism of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) onto the kernel of p and s is a section of p. It then follows from I, 2.3.7 that this sequence of morphisms allows us to identify \(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)\) with the semi-direct product of G by \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\). The corresponding operation

of G on \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) is denoted Ad and called the adjoint representation of G; by definition one thus has \[ \mathrm{Ad}(x)X = i^{-1}(s(x)i(X)s(x)^{-1}), \qquad x \in G(S'),\ X \in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S'). \] If G is commutative, then \(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)\) is too and \(\mathrm{Ad}(x)X = X\).

If G and H are two group functors over S and if \(f : G \to H\) is a group morphism, one deduces from it by functoriality a morphism of exact sequences compatible with the canonical sections: \[ \xymatrix{ 1 \ar[r] & \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M) \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{L}(f)} & \mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M) \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{T}(f)} & G \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{f} & 1 \\ 1 \ar[r] & \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(H/S,M) \ar[r] & \mathrm{T}_{H/S}(M) \ar[r] & H \ar[r] & 1 } \]
\(\mathrm{L}(f)\), which will also be denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(f)\), is the derived morphism of f.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1.1

Let \(g \in G(S)\). Then \(\mathrm{Ad}(g) : \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M) \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) is the derived morphism of \(\mathrm{Int}(g) : G \to G\).
Indeed \(\mathrm{Ad}(g)X = i^{-1}(\mathrm{Int}(g)i(X))\), which is none other than \(\mathrm{L}(\mathrm{Int}(g))(X)\) by the very definition of the derived morphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

Suppose that G/S satisfies (E). Then we know (3.10 cor.1) that the group structure of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) defined as above is none other than the structure induced by its \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module structure (defined thanks to (E)). It follows from the preceding proposition that the \(\mathrm{Ad}(g)\) respect the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module structure of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\): \[ \mathrm{Ad} : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)) \] in other words, Ad is a linear representation of G in the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks

1) For G/S to satisfy (E), it is necessary and sufficient that for every pair (M, N) of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules free of finite type, the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M\oplus N) \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M) \ar[dr] & & \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,N) \ar[dl] \\ & \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,0) = S & } \] obtained by applying the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,\ )\) to the diagram (\(*\)) of 2.1, be cartesian.

2) If G/S satisfies (E), the derived morphism of the group law \(\pi : G\times_S G \to G\) is none other than the addition law in \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\).

blocks.title: Nota
blocks.html:
Nota

\(\pi\) is not a group morphism, but \(\pi(e,e) = e\), which suffices to define \(\mathrm{L}(\pi)\) (cf. 3.7).

Let us now study the set \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)/G)\). Let us first note that one has an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}(G, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)/G) \] defined in the following manner: to \(f : G \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\), one associates the section \(s_f : G \to \mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)\) such that \[ s_f(g) = i(f(g))s(g), \qquad g \in G(S'),\ S' \to S. \]

Let h be an automorphism of the functor G over S, not necessarily respecting the group structure. To every section t of \(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)\), one may associate h(t) defined by transport of structure: it is for example the unique section of \(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)\) making the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ G \ar[r]^-{t} \ar[d]_-{h} & \mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M) \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{T}(h)} \\ G \ar[r]_-{h(t)} & \mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M) } \] commutative.

In particular, let us take for h the right translation by an element x of G(S). \[ h(g) = t_x(g) = g\cdot x, \qquad g \in G(S'),\ S' \to S. \] One has immediately \[ t_x(s_f) = s_{t_x(f)} \] where \(t_x(f)\) denotes the morphism from G to \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) defined by \[ t_x(f)(g) = f(g\cdot x^{-1}), \qquad g \in G(S'),\ S' \to S. \] It follows that if we let G operate by right translations on \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)/G)\) and \(\mathrm{Hom}(G, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M))\), the isomorphism defined above respects the operations of G. In particular the sections of \(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)\) invariant under right translation correspond in this isomorphism to the constant morphisms from G to \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) (i.e. those factoring through the projection \(G \to S\)) or again to the elements of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S)\). In other words:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1.2 (*)

The map \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S) \to \Gamma(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)/G)\) which associates to \(X \in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S)\) the section \(x \mapsto X\cdot x\) is an isomorphism of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S)\) onto the part of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{T}_{G/S}(M)/G)\) formed by the sections invariant under right translation.
Taking 3.12, corollary into account, one obtains:
(*) The statements 4.1.2, 4.1.3 and 4.1.4 are obtained more simply by remarking that the automorphisms of G invariant under right translations are the left translations.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1.3 (*)

There exists an isomorphism, functorial in G, between the set \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S)\) and the set of \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\)-endomorphisms of \(G_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}\) inducing the identity on G and commuting with the right translations of G.
Now taking 3.13 into account:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1.4 (*)

Let G be a group functor over S satisfying (E) with respect to S. The group \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S)\) is identified, functorially in G, with the group of \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\)-automorphisms of \(G_{\mathrm{I}_S(M)}\) inducing the identity on G and commuting with right translations.
One thus recovers (in the case \(M = \mathscr{O}_S\)) one of the classical definitions of the Lie algebra of a group.

Before going further, let us establish some new corollaries to 3.11. We saw in loc. cit. that one has a canonical isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{Y/S}(X, \mathrm{T}_{Y/S}(M)); \] suppose that Y is a group; one then has \(\mathrm{T}_{Y/S}(M) = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)\cdot Y = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)_Y\); from this results an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2

Let \(u : X \to Y\) be a morphism of S-groups. One has a functorial isomorphism \[ \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Z}^1_S(X, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)). \]

(\(\mathrm{Z}^1_S\) denotes the "functor of crossed homomorphisms" defined in a manner analogous to the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}\). One lets X operate on \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)\) by the morphism \[ X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } Y \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{Ad}\ } \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)).) \]

This follows from the isomorphism \[ \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{(Y/S)\text{-gr.}}(X, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)\cdot Y) \] and from the well-known identification of the sections of a semi-direct product with the cocycles of the quotient in the kernel (see for example III, 1.2.2).
Applying 3.10, Corollary 1, one deduces:

blocks.title: Corollary 1
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

If Y/S satisfies (E), one has functorial isomorphisms: \[ \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Z}^1_S(X, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)). \]

blocks.title: Corollary 2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

If X/S satisfies (E), one has functorial isomorphisms \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(X)/S, M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Z}^1_S(X, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(X/S,M)). \]

blocks.title: Corollary 3
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

If Y is commutative, one has a functorial isomorphism \[ \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(X, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)). \]

Let us now consider the case where X and Y are \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules. If Y is an \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module, the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)\) is equipped with a structure of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module deduced from that of Y. Equipped with this structure, it will provisionally be denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}'(Y/S,M)\).

When Y/S satisfies (E), we shall always denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)\) the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)\) equipped with the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module structure defined for every functor satisfying (E). We know that the abelian group structures of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S,M)\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}'(Y/S,M)\) coincide, but it is not a priori the same for the module structures (see a counterexample in Section 6).
One has as an immediate consequence of the preceding corollary:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3

One has a functorial isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{L}^u_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(X,Y)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(X, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}'(Y/S,M)). \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

If X/S satisfies (E), one has functorial isomorphisms: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(X)/S, M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(X, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}'(X/S,M)). \]

Before continuing in this direction, let us examine more closely the relations between Y, \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Y/S)\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}'(Y/S)\). Let us first remark that \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathscr{O}_S/S,M) = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}'(\mathscr{O}_S/S,M) = \mathbf{W}(M)\) (I, 4.6) and that one therefore has a canonical isomorphism \[ d : \mathscr{O}_S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathscr{O}_S/S). \] Now let F be an \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module. For every \(S'\) over S, one has a dihomomorphism, \[ F(S) \longrightarrow F(S') \qquad \mathbf{O}(S) \longrightarrow \mathbf{O}(S'), \] whence a morphism of \(\mathbf{O}(S')\)-modules \[ F(S) \otimes_{\mathbf{O}(S)} \mathbf{O}(S') \longrightarrow F(S'). \] In particular, for \(S' = \mathrm{I}_S(M)\), one deduces morphisms functorial in M \[ F(S) \otimes_{\mathbf{O}(S)} \mathrm{T}_{\mathscr{O}_S/S}(M)(S) \longrightarrow \mathrm{T}_{F/S}(M)(S), \] which, letting S vary, one may write \[ F \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathrm{T}_{\mathscr{O}_S/S}(M) \longrightarrow \mathrm{T}_{F/S}(M). \] These morphisms are functorial in M, hence compatible with the projections of the tangent bundles onto their bases. They then define morphisms \[ F \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathscr{O}_S/S,M) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S,M). \] Let us remark that these last morphisms are functorial in F, and by construction respect the module structures deduced from that of F (what we have called \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}'(F/S,M)\) on the right-hand side). If F/S satisfies (E), since these morphisms are also functorial in M, they also respect the module structures deduced from that of M by means of the condition (E), that is, those denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}\).
Let us note finally that by tensoring with \(d : \mathscr{O}_S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathscr{O}_S/S)\), one deduces a

morphism \[ F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathscr{O}_S/S) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S) \] also denoted \(d : F \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.4

We say that F is a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module if the morphisms \[ F\otimes \mathrm{T}_{\mathscr{O}_S/S}(M) \longrightarrow \mathrm{T}_{F/S}(M) \] are isomorphisms.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

If F is good, then: (i) F/S satisfies (E), (ii) \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S,M) = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}'(F/S,M)\), (iii) \(d : F \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S)\) is an isomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules.

Indeed:

(i) It suffices to check that \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S,M\oplus N) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S,M)\times_S \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S,N)\). Now if F is good, \(F\otimes \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathscr{O}_S/S,M) \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S,M)\) is an isomorphism. It only remains to remark that the property to be proved is true for \(\mathscr{O}_S\) and that \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathscr{O}_S/S,M)\) is isomorphic to the product of a finite number of copies of \(\mathscr{O}_S\), hence \(F\otimes \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathscr{O}_S/S,M)\) isomorphic to the corresponding product of copies of F.

(ii) The isomorphism \(F\otimes \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathscr{O}_S/S,M) \to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S,M)\) respects the two module structures defined above, but they are the same on the left-hand side.

(iii) d is the composite of two isomorphisms.

Examples of good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules: for every quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module \(\mathscr{E}\), the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules \(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{E})\) and \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{E})\) defined in I, 4.6 are good.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5

Let F be a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module. One has a functorial isomorphism \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F)/S, M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F, \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S,M)) \] which respects the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module structures. In particular, one has an isomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F)/S) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F). \]
This follows immediately from 4.3 and from (i), (ii), (iii).
Let us then set the following definition:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.6

We say that the group functor G over S is good if G satisfies the condition (E) with respect to S and if \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module.
Example. — If G is representable, it is good: G satisfies (E) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is of the form \(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{E})\), hence good.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.7

If F is a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module, the S-group \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F)\) is good.

In any case one has an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{T}_{\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F)/S}(M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F, \mathrm{T}_{F/S}(M)) \] if F/S satisfies (E) and if one equips \(\mathrm{T}_{F/S}(M)\) with the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module structure deduced from that of F. If F is good, then it satisfies (E) and one has isomorphisms \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F, \mathrm{T}_{F/S}(M)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F, F\otimes \mathrm{T}_{\mathscr{O}_S/S}(M)). \] It follows first of all that \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F)\) satisfies (E) with respect to S. One has on the other hand \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F)/S) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F) \] and this last module is good if F is good (for \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathscr{O}_S/S}(M)\) is "free of finite type" over \(\mathscr{O}_S\)).

Now let G be an S-group and F a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module. Suppose given a linear representation of G in F, that is (I, 4.7.1), a morphism of S-groups \[ \rho : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F). \] One deduces from it by 4.5 a morphism \[ \rho' : \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F) \] which (if G/S satisfies (E)) is a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules.

Let in particular G be a good S-group. Then \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module, and one has a morphism of S-groups \[ \mathrm{Ad} : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)). \] One deduces from it by the preceding construction a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules \[ \mathrm{ad} : \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)), \] or, what amounts to the same, a bilinear morphism: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S) \times_S \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S), \] which will be denoted \((x,y) \mapsto [x,y] = \mathrm{ad}(x)\cdot y\) (where x and y denote two arbitrary elements of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)(S') = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G_{S'}/S')(S')\)). If G is commutative, then \([x,y]=0\).

One may give an equivalent definition of the bracket as follows: let us first remark that it suffices to do so for \(x,y \in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)(S)\). Let us next remark that there is a canonical isomorphism \(\mathrm{I}_S\times_S \mathrm{I}_S \simeq \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{I}_S}\); to avoid confusion, let us denote by I and \(I'\) two copies of \(\mathrm{I}_S\). One then has a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ I\times I' \ar[r] \ar[d] & I \ar[d] \\ I' \ar[r] & S } \] the two arrows starting from \(I\times I'\) identifying it with the scheme of dual numbers over I or over \(I'\). From this results a commutative diagram of groups (where we write \(\mathrm{L} = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\)):

\[ \xymatrix{ & & 1 \ar[d] & 1 \ar[d] & \\ & & \mathrm{L}(I) \ar[d] & \mathrm{L}(S) \ar[d] & \\ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{L}(I') \ar[r] & G(I\times I') \ar[r] \ar[d] & G(I') \ar[r] \ar[d] & 1 \\ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{L}(S) \ar[r] & G(I) \ar[r] \ar[d] & G(S) \ar[r] \ar[d] & 1 \\ & & 1 & 1 & } \]

The ninth piece of the puzzle is none other than \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{L}/S)(S)\). If G is good, this is \(\mathrm{L}(S)\) and one thus has a commutative diagram in which the rows and columns are exact sequences of groups and in which the five \(\mathrm{L}(\ )\) are commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ z\in \mathrm{L}(S) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{L}(I) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{L}(S) \ni x \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{L}(I') \ar[r] \ar[d] & G(I\times I') \ar[r] \ar[d] & G(I') \ar[d] \\ y\in \mathrm{L}(S) \ar[r] & G(I) \ar[r] & G(S) } \]

Now in such a diagram, if one takes two elements x and y as indicated, and lifts them arbitrarily to \(x'\) and \(y'\) in \(G(I\times I')\), the commutator \(x'y'x'^{-1}y'^{-1}\) of the two elements obtained does not depend on the chosen liftings and is the image of an element z as indicated. The reader will check that one has \(z = [x,y]\) (*).
(*) The editor acknowledges, at Gabriel's request, that the exercise is not immediate; this is moreover the reason why it is not in the text.
From this construction the two following properties are apparent:

(i) the bracket is "functorial in G": more precisely, \(G \mapsto \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is a functor from the category of good S-groups to the category of good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules equipped with a bilinear composition law.
(ii) One has \([x,y]+[y,x] = 0\): indeed the diagram is symmetric with respect to the main diagonal.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.8

Let F be a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module. In the identification \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F)/S) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F) \]

\(\mathrm{Ad}(x)\cdot X\) is transformed into \(x\circ X\circ x^{-1}\) and \([X,Y]\) into \(X\circ Y - Y\circ X\).

Let us first consider \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{I}_S}\text{-mod.}}(F_{\mathrm{I}_S})\). An element x of this group is, by definition of base change for functors, the data, for every \(S' \to \mathrm{I}_S\), of an \(\mathbf{O}(S')\)-automorphism of \(F(S')\), say \(x_{S'}\). Now let us remark that giving an \(S' \to \mathrm{I}_S\) is equivalent to giving an \(S' \to S\) equipped with a section \(s : S' \to \mathrm{I}_{S'}\). One then sees at once that \(x_{S'}\) is determined by \(x_{\mathrm{I}_{S'}}\) and that it therefore suffices, in order to check the required formulas, to check them on the \(F(\mathrm{I}_{S'})\).

Now \(F(\mathrm{I}_{S'}) = F(S')\cdot \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S)(S') = F(S')\cdot F(S')\) since F is good. Let us write \(\mathbf{O}(\mathrm{I}_{S'}) = \mathbf{O}(S')[\varepsilon]\), \(\varepsilon^2=0\). It is immediate to see that the endomorphism X of F corresponds under the given identification to the automorphism \(\mathrm{I}+\varepsilon X\) of \(F(\mathrm{I}_{S'})\). It only remains to translate the definitions in order to reduce the announced formulas to \[ x\circ(\mathrm{I}+\varepsilon X)\circ x^{-1} = \mathrm{I} + \varepsilon(x\circ X\circ x^{-1}) \] and \[ (\mathrm{I}+\varepsilon X)\circ(\mathrm{I}+\varepsilon' Y)\circ(\mathrm{I}+\varepsilon X)^{-1}\circ(\mathrm{I}+\varepsilon' Y)^{-1} = \mathrm{I} + \varepsilon\varepsilon'(X\circ Y - Y\circ X) \] when \(\varepsilon^2 = \varepsilon'^2 = 0\).

blocks.title: Corollary 1
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

Let G be a good S-group and \(x,y,z \in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)(S')\). One has: \[ [x,[y,z]] + [y,[z,x]] + [z,[x,y]] = 0. \]

Indeed, if G is good, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module and the morphism of S-groups \(\mathrm{Ad} : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S))\) gives by functoriality \[ [\mathrm{ad}\,x, \mathrm{ad}\,y] = \mathrm{ad}\,x\circ \mathrm{ad}\,y - \mathrm{ad}\,y\circ \mathrm{ad}\,x = \mathrm{ad}[x,y], \] which, applied to an element z, gives the Jacobi identity.

blocks.title: Corollary 2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let G be a good S-group operating linearly on a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module F (i.e. let F be a G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module, G and F good). Then the linear map \(\rho' : \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S) \to \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F)\) is a representation, that is, one has \[ \rho'([x,y]) = \rho'(x)\circ \rho'(y) - \rho'(y)\circ \rho'(x). \]

blocks.title: Scholium
blocks.html:
Scholium 4.9

To every good S-group (for example a representable one), we have associated a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) functorially equipped with a bilinear map satisfying \[ [x,y]+[y,x]=0, \qquad [x,[y,z]]+[y,[z,x]]+[z,[x,y]]=0. \] We shall call \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) equipped with this structure the "Lie algebra" of G over S (the quotation marks being justified by the fact that \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is not strictly speaking an \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Lie algebra). To every linear representation of G in a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module F is associated a representation of its "Lie algebra". In particular, to the adjoint representation of G is associated the adjoint representation of its "Lie algebra".

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.10

A group functor G over S is said to be very good if it is good and if \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is an \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Lie algebra (that is, if one has identically \([x,x]=0\)).

Examples of very good S-groups. The group \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F)\) for every good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module F. Every representable group (see below). Every good S-group admitting a monomorphism into a very good S-group, for example every good subgroup functor of a representable group, or every good S-group admitting a faithful linear representation in a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module, for example every good S-group such that Ad is a monomorphism…

Suppose now that G is a group scheme over S. By 4.1.4, \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)(S)\) is identified with the group of right-invariant infinitesimal automorphisms of G/S, that is, by 3.14, with the group of derivations of \(\mathscr{O}_G\) over \(\mathscr{O}_S\) invariant under right translation. Moreover this identification respects the module structure and even the Lie algebra structure, the bracket in \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)(S)\) corresponding to the bracket of derivations, as one sees by reasoning as in Proposition 4.8: one must compute the commutator of the automorphisms u and v of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{\mathrm{I}_S\times \mathrm{I}_S}}\) defined by \[ u = \mathrm{I}+\varepsilon d, \qquad v = \mathrm{I}+\varepsilon' d'; \qquad \varepsilon^2 = \varepsilon'^2 = 0. \] One finds immediately \(uvu^{-1}v^{-1} = \mathrm{I}+\varepsilon\varepsilon'(dd'-d'd)\).

One then recovers the classical definition: \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is the functor which, to every \(S'\) over S, associates the \(\mathbf{O}(S')\)-Lie algebra of derivations of \(G_{S'}\) with respect to \(S'\) invariant under right translation. It follows in particular that every representable group is very good.

Let us recall on the other hand (3.3) that \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is representable by the vector bundle \(\mathbf{V}(\omega^1_{G/S})\), where \(\omega^1_{G/S}\) is the inverse image under the unit section of G of the sheaf \(\Omega^1_{G/S}\) of relative differentials of G with respect to S. The preceding properties show that the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module \(\omega^1_{G/S}\) is identified with the sheaf of right-invariant differentials of G with respect to S, that is, the sheaf whose sections over an open subset U of S are the sections of \(\Omega^1_{G/S}\) over the inverse image of U invariant under right translation.

Let us note finally that it follows from the decomposition of the tangent bundle into a semi-direct product that the \(\mathscr{O}_G\)-Module \(\Omega^1_{G/S}\) is the inverse image under the projection \(G\to S\) of the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module \(\omega^1_{G/S}\). It follows for example that \(\Omega^1_{G/S}\) is locally free (resp. locally free of finite type) if \(\omega^1_{G/S}\) is, which is in particular the case if S is the spectrum of a field (resp. if S is the spectrum of a field and G locally of finite type over S).

We have thus associated functorially to every S-group scheme G over S a vector bundle \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) over S equipped with a structure of S-scheme in Lie algebras over the S-scheme of rings \(\mathscr{O}_S\). Let us recall (3.4 and 3.8) that this construction commutes with finite products and with base extension.

Let \(\mathscr{L}ie(G/S)\) be the sheaf of sections of this vector bundle (EGA II 1.7.9). It is equipped with a structure of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Lie Algebra. Since this construction does not commute with base extension (in general), the Lie Algebra structure on this Module does not allow one to reconstruct the structure of scheme in Lie algebras on \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\). It is however the case when \(\omega^1_{G/S}\) is locally free of finite type (which happens

in particular if G is smooth over S or if S is the spectrum of a field and G locally of finite type over S), for one then has \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S) = \mathbf{V}(\omega^1_{G/S}) = \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}ie(G/S)). \qquad (\text{I, 4.6.5, Cor.}) \]

Let us note finally that if \(G\to H\) is a monomorphism of group functors, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(H/S)\) is also a monomorphism. If G and H are representable, then \(\omega^1_{H/S}\to \omega^1_{G/S}\) is an epimorphism and \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(H/S)\) is a closed immersion; indeed a vectorial monomorphism of vector bundles is a closed immersion. One has in particular the following result: let \(G\to H\) be a monomorphism of group schemes over S; if \(\omega^1_{G/S}\) is locally free of finite type, then the corresponding morphism \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(H/S)\) is an isomorphism of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) onto a submodule of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(H/S)\) which is locally a direct factor.
5. Computation of some Lie algebras
5.1. Examples of Lie algebras: diagonalizable groups.

Let \(G = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\) be a diagonalizable group over S (I, 4.4). Since the formation of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) commutes with base extension, it suffices to carry out the construction for \(G=\mathbf{D}(M)\). One then has: \[ G(\mathrm{I}_S) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M,\Gamma(\mathrm{I}_S,\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{I}_S})^{\times}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M,\Gamma(S,\mathrm{D}_{\mathscr{O}_S})^{\times}). \] Now one has a split exact sequence \[ 1 \longrightarrow \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S) \longrightarrow \Gamma(S,\mathrm{D}_{\mathscr{O}_S})^{\times} \longrightarrow \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^{\times} \longrightarrow 1, \] which gives \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G)(S) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M,\mathbf{O}(S)) \] equipped with its evident \(\mathbf{O}(S)\)-module structure. One therefore obtains after base change:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1

One has isomorphisms \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(M_S,\mathscr{O}_S) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathbf{D}_S(M)/S) \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathscr{H}om(\underline{M}_S,\mathscr{O}_S) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathscr{L}ie(\mathbf{D}_S(M)/S), \] (where, in the second isomorphism, \(\underline{M}_S\) denotes the constant sheaf of groups on S defined by M, and \(\mathscr{H}om\) the sheaf of homomorphisms of sheaves of groups).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

If M is free of finite type (or, as we shall say later, if \(\mathbf{D}_S(M)\) is a split torus) then \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}ie(\mathbf{D}_S(M)/S)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathbf{D}_S(M)/S) \quad \text{(see I, 4.6.5),} \qquad M^{\vee}_S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathscr{O}_S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathscr{L}ie(\mathbf{D}_S(M)/S), \] where \(M^{\vee}\) denotes the dual of the abelian group M. In particular \[ \mathscr{O}_S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathbb{G}_{m,S}/S) \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathscr{O}_S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathscr{L}ie(\mathbb{G}_{m,S}/S). \]
5.2. Normalizers and centralizers.
Let us first prove some lemmas on \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.1

A sequence \(0\to F'\to F\to F''\to 0\) of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules is exact if and only if for every \(S'\) over S the sequence \(0\to F'(S')\to F(S')\to F''(S')\to 0\) of \(\mathbf{O}(S')\)-modules is exact.

The sequences \(0\to \mathrm{T}_{F'/S}(M)\to \mathrm{T}_{F/S}(M)\to \mathrm{T}_{F''/S}(M)\to 0\) and \(0\to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F'/S,M)\to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S,M)\to \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F''/S,M)\to 0\) are then exact. If two of the modules involved are good, so is the third. If the sequences \(0\to F'\to F\to F''\to 0\) and \(0\to H'\to H\to H''\to 0\) are exact, then the sequence \(0\to F'\times_S H'\to F\times_S H\to F''\times_S H''\to 0\) is exact as well.

The proofs (immediate) are left to the reader (for the next-to-last assertion, use the five lemma and the fact that \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathscr{O}_S/S}(M)\) is free).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.2

Let F and H be two G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules. The canonical homomorphism \(F^G\times_S H^G \to (F\times_S H)^G\) is an isomorphism. If F is good, so is \(F^G\).

Let us prove the second assertion. One has a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ F^G(\mathrm{I}_S(M)) \ar[r] & F(\mathrm{I}_S(M)) \\ F^G(S)\otimes_{\mathbf{O}(S)} \mathbf{O}(\mathrm{I}_S(M)) \ar[r] \ar[u]^-{f} & F(S)\otimes_{\mathbf{O}(S)} \mathbf{O}(\mathrm{I}_S(M)) \ar[u]_-{\simeq,\,f_1} } \] and one must prove that f is bijective; now it is evidently injective; let us show that it is surjective. So let \(u\in F(S)\otimes \mathbf{O}(\mathrm{I}_S(M))\) be such that \(f_1(u)\) is invariant under \(G(\mathrm{I}_S(M))\). Let us consider S as being over \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\) by the zero section. If \(S'\) is over S, then \(u\in F(S)^{G(S')}\otimes \mathbf{O}(\mathrm{I}_S(M))\), \(\mathbf{O}(\mathrm{I}_S(M))\) being free over \(\mathbf{O}(S)\). Letting \(S'\) vary, one deduces that \(u\in F^G(S)\otimes \mathbf{O}(\mathrm{I}_S(M))\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.2.3

Let G be an S-group and K a sub-S-group of G. Write \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(K) = N\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(K) = Z\) (I, 2.3.3). Let K operate on \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) by means of the adjoint representation of G.

(i) If the group law of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) is commutative (*) then \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(N/S,M)\big/\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S,M) = \bigl(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\big/\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S,M)\bigr)^K \] (if E and F are two commutative S-groups, we denote by E/F the commutative S-group defined by \((E/F)(S') = E(S')/F(S')\)).

(ii) If the group law of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) is commutative (*), then \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(Z/S,M) = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)^K. \]

(iii) If G satisfies (E) (resp. if G and K satisfy (E)), then Z satisfies (E) (resp. N satisfies (E)).

(iv) If G is good (resp. if G and K are good), then Z is good (resp. N is good). If moreover G is very good, then Z (resp. N) is very good.
(*) Condition automatically satisfied if G satisfies (E) (cf. 3.9), for example if G is representable.

Let us prove (i) and (ii). Let H = N (resp. Z). One has \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(H/S,M)(S) = \{X\in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S)\subset G(\mathrm{I}_S(M)) \mid X\cdot u\cdot X^{-1}\cdot u^{-1}\in K(S')\ (\text{resp.}\ =1)\text{ for all } u\in K(S'),\ S'\longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_S(M)\}. \] Let \(X\in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S)\). For X to belong to \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(H/S,M)(S)\), it suffices to check the preceding condition for all \(S'\) of the form \(\mathrm{I}_{S_1}(M)\), with structural morphism arising by base extension from a morphism \(S_1\to S\). Indeed one has a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathrm{I}_{S'}(M) \ar[d]^-{h} \\ \mathrm{I}_S(M) \ar[ur] \ar[d] & S' \ar[dl] \\ S & } \] which shows that giving an \(S'\) over \(\mathrm{I}_S(M)\) is equivalent to giving an \(S'\) over S equipped with a section h of \(\mathrm{I}_{S'}(M)\) over \(S'\); now if the required property is true for every u in \(K(\mathrm{I}_{S'}(M))\), it will also be true for every \(u\in K(S')\) since \(K(h) : K(\mathrm{I}_{S'}(M)) \to K(S')\) is surjective.

Now every \(u\in K(\mathrm{I}_{S'}(M))\) can be written uniquely as \(Y\cdot k\) where \(k\in K(S')\) and \(Y\in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S,M)(S')\). The expression \(X\cdot u\cdot X^{-1}\cdot u^{-1}\) then becomes \(XYkX^{-1}k^{-1}Y^{-1}\) which, if \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)\) is commutative, can be written \(X\,\mathrm{Ad}(k)(X^{-1})\). But the latter is a priori in \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)(S')\). The condition on X is therefore written \(X\,\mathrm{Ad}(k)(X^{-1})\in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S,M)(S')\) (resp. \(=1\)) for all \(k\in K(S')\). This proves (ii). In the case \(H=N\), if we denote by \(\dot{X}\) the image of X in F(S) (where we set \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S,M)/\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S,M) = F\)), the condition is written \(\mathrm{Ad}(k)(\overline{X}^{-1}) = \overline{X}^{-1}\) where \(\overline{X}\) is the image of X in \(F(S')\), which proves (i).
(iii) is then proved immediately. (iv) follows from Lemmas 5.2.1 and 5.2.2.

blocks.title: Corollary 1
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

One has \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(G)/S) = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)^G\) if the group law of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is commutative.

blocks.title: Corollary 2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

If the group law of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) is commutative and if K is a normal subgroup of G, then \[ \bigl(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\big/\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S)\bigr)^K = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\big/\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S). \]
5.3. Linear representations.

Let G be a good S-group operating linearly on a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module F \[ \rho : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F). \] We have defined (4.8, cor. 2) a corresponding linear representation \[ \rho' : \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(F). \] The sub-S-groups \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(E)\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(E)\) are defined for every subset E of F, for example for every sub-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module E of F. One sets in an analogous manner \[ \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)}(E)(S') = \{X\in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)(S'),\ \rho'(X)E_{S'}\subset E_{S'}\}. \] \[ \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)}(E)(S') = \{X\in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)(S'),\ \rho'(X)E_{S'} = 0\}. \] (Note that this construction can be carried out for every linear representation of an \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-"Lie algebra" and that the two subobjects constructed are sub-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules stable under the bracket).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.1

Let G be a good S-group operating linearly on a good \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module F, and E a sub-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module of F.

(i) One has \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(E)/S) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)}(E), \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(E)/S) = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)}(E). \]

(ii) \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(E)\) is good; if E is good, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(E)\) is good. If moreover G is very good, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(E)\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(E)\) are very good.

The proof is easy and left to the reader.

This applies in particular to the case where one takes for \(\rho\) the adjoint representation of G. To every submodule E of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) one thus associates two subgroups of G, its centralizer and its normalizer, whose Lie algebras are respectively the centralizer and the normalizer of E in \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\), computed as usual by means of the bracket: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)}(E)(S') = \{X\in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)(S'),\ [X,E_{S'}]\subset E_{S'}\}. \] \[ \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)}(E)(S') = \{X\in \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)(S'),\ [X,E_{S'}] = 0\}. \]

Let then K be a sub-S-group of G. Then \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S)\) is a sub-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\) and one evidently has \[ \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(K) \subset \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S)) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(K) \subset \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S)), \] whence \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(K)/S) \subset \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)}(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S)) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(K)/S) \subset \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)}(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S)), \] but none of these four inclusions is a priori an identity; we shall see many examples of this in the sequel.
It follows in particular from these inclusions that if K is a normal subgroup of G, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(K/S)\) is an ideal of \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(G/S)\).
6. Miscellaneous remarks

One may define the bracket of two infinitesimal automorphisms for an S-functor X which is not necessarily a group. It suffices to apply the results of this exposé to the group \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(X)\). To arrive at a pleasant formalism, one is led to suppose X good, that is, to suppose that the \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-module \(\mathrm{T}_{X/S}\) is good (if X is an S-group, this definition evidently coincides with Definition 4.6).

There exist functors possessing infinitesimal endomorphisms which are not automorphisms, hence a fortiori not satisfying the condition (E). Take for example for X(S) the free abelian monoid generated by the elements of \(\mathbf{O}(S)\). Each morphism \(S\to \mathrm{I}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) defines an element of square zero of \(\mathbf{O}(S)\), say u, hence an endomorphism of X(S) by \(x\mapsto x+u\) (sum in the free monoid). It is immediate that one has thus constructed an endomorphism of \(X_{\mathrm{I}_{\mathbb{Z}}}\). If \(S\to \mathrm{I}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) factors through the zero section of \(\mathrm{I}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), then this endomorphism is the identity. It is therefore an infinitesimal endomorphism of X which is evidently not an automorphism.

There exist modules which are not good. One may for example modify slightly the preceding counterexample, but one may also give one as follows: let \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\), k a field of characteristic p, and let E be a vector space over k of finite dimension. Consider the vector bundle \(\mathbf{W}(E)\) and equip it with the \(\mathbf{O}(S)\)-module structure obtained by letting the scalars operate through the p-th power. Denote by F the \(\mathbf{O}(S)\)-module obtained. It is representable, hence satisfies (E). Its Lie algebra is evidently \(\mathbf{W}(E)\) and the canonical morphism \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(F/S)\to F\) does respect the abelian group structure (it could hardly do otherwise) but not the module structure. The \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module F is therefore not good, although it is representable.

Let G be a group functor over S. One has by definition the following implications \[ (\text{G/S satisfies (E)}) \Longleftarrow (\text{G is good}) \Longleftarrow (\text{G is very good}). \] It would be interesting to prove or to give counterexamples to the implications in the opposite direction.
